subcategory: Gulf Coast native spring iris-family corm

planting.note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple pleat-leaf is native to the moist prairies, roadsides, and open grassy areas of the Texas Gulf Coast and adjacent Louisiana, where it blooms in March–May in dense spring colonies. Each individual flower lasts only one day but the plant produces a long succession of blooms. Goes dormant in summer heat. The pleated, iris-like leaves are distinctive.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple pleat-leaf is a charming, small native iris-family wildflower of the Texas Gulf Coast — one of those overlooked native plants that is easily passed by in a nursery but produces, in its natural prairie colonies in March and April, an unexpectedly beautiful display of small, brilliant, deep-blue-violet to purple, three-outer-petaled flowers (with white and yellow markings at the throat of each outer petal) that stop observers in their tracks with the intense, jewel-like quality of the color against the pale spring prairie grass. Each individual flower lasts only a single day but the colony produces a sustained succession of blooms over 4–6 weeks. The narrow, pleated, iris-like leaves (giving both the common name 'pleat-leaf' and the family resemblance) emerge in late winter. Native to the Texas coastal prairies from the Houston area to Corpus Christi and adjacent Louisiana, it is precisely adapted to zone 9a Gulf Coast conditions of warm-wet springs, hot-dry summers, and mild winters. An underused Gulf Coast native for spring prairie and naturalistic garden plantings.

planting.note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; prairie celestial is native to the dry, rocky, and sandy grasslands and open woodlands of Texas, Oklahoma, Kansas, and Missouri, where it blooms in April–June on limestone prairies and sandy post oak savanna. The flowers last only a single morning — opening at dawn and closing by midday — but the plant produces a succession of blooms. The corms multiply to form colonies over time.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; prairie celestial is one of the most beautiful native wildflowers of the Texas, Oklahoma, and Missouri prairies — a small iris-family plant producing in late spring large (for the plant's size), brilliant, sky-blue to blue-violet, six-petaled, open-faced flowers that in form closely resemble small blue irises without the typical iris falls and standards differentiation — the six petals are more nearly equal in size, creating a flat, open, circular flower of pure, clean blue with yellow and white markings at the center throat. The flowers are 'ephemeral' in the literal sense — each individual bloom opens at dawn and is closed and wilted by midday, having lasted only a single morning — but the plant produces a succession of blooms over several weeks, and a naturalized colony in bloom can produce dozens of the pure-blue morning flowers at once. The name 'celestial' perfectly captures the sky-blue color. Native to the rocky limestone prairie, sandy post oak savanna, and open woodlands of the central US, it is an outstanding native plant for prairie garden and naturalistic meadow plantings in zones 5–9.

subcategory: South American–naturalized Gulf Coast iris relative

planting.note: Plant small corms 2 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie iris has naturalized extensively on the Texas Gulf Coast, roadsides, and open areas, blooming in spring and early summer. It is originally from South America (Argentina, Uruguay) but has naturalized so thoroughly in coastal Texas that it is now treated ecologically as part of the local flora. Each blue flower lasts only a single day. The corms multiply quickly to form colonies.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie iris is a South American iris-family corm that has naturalized so extensively on the Texas Gulf Coast — roadsides, vacant lots, suburban lawns, open fields — that it has become a characteristic and beloved part of the Gulf Coast spring flora, blooming from March through May in flushes of sky-blue, six-petaled, open-faced flowers with yellow throats, each flower lasting a single day but the colony producing successive blooms over weeks. The flowers are slightly smaller than those of Nemastylus geminiflora but similarly pure blue-violet with yellow center markings, and the plant is significantly more adaptable and self-seeding than the native prairie irises, spreading freely in disturbed and maintained areas of coastal Texas. For Gulf Coast gardeners in zone 9a, calydorea has already naturalized in their region — it is familiar as a spring roadside wildflower — and including it in native prairie and naturalistic garden plantings supports the locally established population of this spring blue. A prolific, low-maintenance, self-sustaining small bulb that, once established, provides years of spring color without any attention.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; pale yellow-eyed grass is larger and bolder than the blue-flowered North American sisyrinchiums — it forms iris-like fans of gray-green, sword-shaped leaves and produces in late spring–early summer multiple upright flower spikes of small, pale creamy-yellow, six-petaled flowers with purple veining. It self-sows freely and can form substantial naturalized clumps. Short-lived perennial that maintains itself by seeding.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; pale yellow-eyed grass is a significantly larger and more architectural plant than the familiar blue-flowered North American sisyrinchiums — it forms bold, iris-like fans of flat, sword-shaped, gray-green leaves (distinctly iris-like in form and arrangement, unlike the grass-like leaves of smaller sisyrinchiums) and in late spring through early summer produces multiple, upright, stiffly erect flower spikes of small, cup-shaped, pale cream to soft primrose-yellow flowers with delicate purple veining, packed along the upper portions of the stem in the manner of a slender poker lily. The effect is refined and distinctive — pale, cream-yellow flowers on architectural gray-green sword-leaf fans — both restrained and elegant in the border. A short-lived perennial that replenishes itself freely from self-sown seed in open, well-drained, disturbed soil — functioning practically as a self-maintaining garden plant in zones 5–8. The cultivar 'Aunt May' (variegated cream-striped leaves) is widely available and more commonly grown than the type. Native to Argentina and Chile; naturalized in parts of western Europe.

subcategory: multi-stemmed South African Cape corn lily

planting.note: Plant small corms 3–4 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; many-spiked corn lily follows the Cape winter-rainfall growing cycle — emerging in autumn, blooming in late winter and spring, then going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer. The species name 'polystachya' (many-spiked) reflects its tendency to produce multiple branching flower stems from a single corm, providing more flowers than single-stemmed corn lily species.

directions: Plant corms in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; many-spiked corn lily is a South African Cape Ixia that distinguishes itself from other ixias by producing multiple branching flower spikes from each corm — the 'polystachya' habit — creating a more complex, airy, branched flower display rather than the single wand of most corn lily species. The flowers are typical ixia form — small, star-shaped, six-petaled, in white to pale pink to pale violet with a dark center, clustered along the upper portions of very slender, wiry, arching stems. The collective effect of a planting of I. polystachya in full spring bloom is an airy, wiry cloud of small, starry flowers above narrow, grass-like leaves — a delicate, finely textured spring display of considerable charm. Like all Cape Ixia species, it follows the strict Mediterranean-climate cycle of autumn emergence, winter and spring growth and flowering, complete summer dormancy, and requires the corresponding dry summer period in cultivation. An excellent mixing bulb planted through drought-tolerant perennials and grasses in zone 9–11 Mediterranean-climate and Gulf Coast gardens, particularly in the sandy, well-drained soils of coastal areas.
